text: This video is related to a young lady with a rectovaginal fistula after a vaginal delivery.
She had a third degree tear repaired intraoperatively.
Unfortunately, she experienced wound dehiscence with fecal incontinence and vaginal discharge of feces ten days after the delivery.
The surgical team performs a colostomy to address the defect associated with the rectovaginal fistula.
After this, a rectovaginal fistula was diagnosed.
The video shows the intact anal sphincter mechanism, with no visible signs of damage or disruption.
As part of the study of the hair symptoms, we performed a pelvic ultrasound, which showed a less of one quadrant defect on the external anal sphincter and two quadrant defects on the internal one.
As you know, there is no single technique of repair appropriated for all rectovaginal fistulas.
The key is the assessment of anal sphincter function.
Following the American guidelines for the management of rectovaginal fistula, there are different available techniques.

The surgeon demonstrates the rectal advancement flap technique, providing direct access to the high-pressure side of the rectum. The flap is positioned carefully to minimize the risk of enlarging the fistula if the procedure fails.
The vaginal approach is easier and allows a rectocele repair if required at the same time.
Talking about Transperineal Repair is essentially to a conversion to a fourth degree tear, which is then repaired.
Mostly, it's used when there is an extensive injury to both sphincters.
Related to the Marshall's flap, it's usually used only after failure of previous repairs, when the dissection of a scarred tissue plane is difficult.
The surgeon mobilizes the labial fat pad and creates a tunnel leading to the rectovaginal septum, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.

The surgeon performs a transparent nail approach using a posterior fourchette incision, directly repairing rectal and vaginal wall defects. An anterior lobartoplasty is conducted to interpose healthy tissue, enhancing the consistency of the tissue repair.
Preoperatively, patients have a rectal enema and receive a dose of prophylactic antibiotic.
The surgeon infiltrates the surgical area with local anesthetic, which aids in visually identifying tissue planes during the dissection process.
As planned, we perform the procedure on lithophilic position.
The surgeon uses an Eisenheimer retractor to expose the anal canal, then applies betadine with a swab held by Russian forceps to clean the area.
A swab with an attached proline stitch is placed in the anal canal to prevent fecal soiling during the procedure.
The surgeon examines the anal region, confirming the presence of a fistula with a two-centimeter diameter located above the sphincter, deemed suitable for primary repair.
The surgeon infiltrates the surgical area with a local anesthetic combined with adrenaline, preparing the tissue for dissection by enhancing visibility of tissue planes.
The surgeon makes a transverse incision at the posterior fourchette along the mucocutaneous junction of the vagina.

As our group published previously, the posterior fourchette approach facilitates early wound closure and the risk of wound infection is reduced in comparison to the conventional perineal approach anteriorly to the anal verge.
The surgeon opens the rectovaginal septum and begins dissecting the mucosal plane to address the fistula defect, revealing the tissue layers involved in the repair process.
The surgeon performs a circumferential incision around the vaginal opening of the fistula, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon begins dissection on one side of the fistula, carefully separating tissue while leaving the central part of the fistula for later dissection.
The surgeon controls venous bleeding using diathermy and stitches, despite the application of local anesthetic with adrenaline during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the vaginal mucosa to improve exposure of the surgical area, facilitating access to the distal edge of the fistula for further dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the mucosa plane, progressing towards overcoming the fistula defect in the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in dissecting the area due to fibrosis, which is visually evident as dense tissue resulting from previous inflammation.
There is a high risk of creating a new fistula or a new hole on the vagina.
The surgeon uses medicine-bound scissors to maintain the dissection plane, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers during the procedure.
The assistant provides good traction and contra-traction using surgical instruments, including alleys and suction, to facilitate the dissection process.
The surgeon performs digital contra-traction to maintain the correct dissection plane, ensuring precise tissue manipulation during the procedure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection above the fibrotic area, carefully separating tissue until approximately two centimeters of healthy tissue is exposed above the distal edge of the fistula.
The surgeon identifies the fistula on the vaginal side, visually confirming its location in relation to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a primary repair of the fistula using two interrupted ovicole stitches, carefully placing them to ensure proper closure of the tissue.
In this case, it's not recommended to perform an endovaginal flap due to the fistula is too big.
The surgeon identifies the levator muscle on the lateral side of the space opened between the vagina and rectum, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
Evidence shows that Lebertoplasty, when the adjacent tissues are easily opposable, or a martial flap, when they are more rigid, strengthen the primary suture.
The surgeon performs anterior Lebertoplasty using full-thickness technique, placing two OPDS interrupted stitches while carefully avoiding the rectal and vaginal walls.
The surgeon performs sequential sutures during Lebertoplasty, progressing naturally towards the sphincter area, which can be visually assessed for potential plication or repair.
As you know, for repairing the anal sphincter we need to divide the external sphincter, which in our passion is almost complete.
The surgeon examines the anal sphincter intraoperatively, observing that it is complete and consistent, indicating that no repair is necessary.
The surgeon places a drain in the rectovaginal space to prevent the formation of hematomas, ensuring proper drainage and reducing the risk of fluid accumulation.
We leave the drain no more than twenty-four hours.
The surgeon begins closing the vaginal mucosa using a taper cut needle, which is recommended due to the toughness of the vaginal tissue.
The surgeon closes the vaginal mucosa using a continuous locking suture technique with Vigil suture, ensuring the vagina is not shortened during the procedure.
The surgeon closes the mucocutaneous incision using III-O Vicryl suture, completing the perineal wound closure with a posterior fourchette incision.
Thank you very much for your attention
